title: 23_cors_preflight.jsx
descriptionMd:
## Mission: The Fetch Is Blocked — CORS Error in the Console

A dashboard at `http://localhost:3000` fetches weather data from a public API at `https://api.weather-service.example`. The request works perfectly in a Python script or with curl — but the browser blocks it with a CORS error:

```
Access to fetch at 'https://api.weather-service.example/current'
from origin 'http://localhost:3000' has been blocked by CORS policy:
No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header is present on the target resource.
```

CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a **browser security feature**. The API server controls whether your app is allowed to read its responses. The fix is a server-side header change — not a frontend workaround.
traceback:
Browser console:
  Access to fetch at 'https://api.weather-service.example/current'
  from origin 'http://localhost:3000' has been blocked by CORS policy:
  No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header is present on the target resource.

  TypeError: Failed to fetch

Browser Network tab:
  OPTIONS https://api.weather-service.example/current  CORS Error
  (or) GET request shows (blocked) with 0 bytes received

UI: Error: Failed to fetch
correctOutput:
Browser Network tab:
  GET https://api.weather-service.example/current  200 OK  84ms
  Response headers: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:3000

UI: Current temperature: 18°C

options.label: Add `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` on the API server (or route through your own backend)
options.resultLog:
With Access-Control-Allow-Origin header on the API server:
  GET https://api.weather-service.example/current  200 OK
  Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:3000
  → Browser allows the response through. UI renders temperature.

With a backend proxy at /api/weather:
  Browser fetches /api/weather (same origin, no CORS)
  Your server fetches from api.weather-service.example (server-to-server, no CORS)
  → Proxy returns data to browser. UI renders temperature.
options.rationale: CORS is enforced by the **browser**, based on response headers from the **server**. The only real fixes are: (1) the target API adds `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: your-domain` to its responses — requires access to the API server; (2) you create a backend proxy that fetches the API server-to-server and returns the data through your own domain — server-to-server requests have no CORS restriction. The frontend JavaScript code is fine; the block happens at the browser's network layer after the response arrives.

options.label: Add `mode: 'no-cors'` to the fetch options
options.resultLog:
Browser Network tab:
  GET https://api.weather-service.example/current  200 OK (opaque)
  Response body: (empty — opaque response)

Browser console:
  Uncaught SyntaxError: Unexpected end of JSON input
  (r.json() on an opaque response returns empty body)

UI: Error: Unexpected end of JSON input

`no-cors` sends the request but hides the response — you can't read the data.
options.rationale: `mode: 'no-cors'` sounds like it bypasses the CORS restriction, but it doesn't — it creates an **opaque response**. An opaque response is a response the browser intentionally hides: `response.ok` is always `false`, `response.status` is `0`, and `response.json()` fails because the body is empty. You sent the request; the browser received the response; but you can't read it. `no-cors` is only useful for fire-and-forget requests (like a tracking pixel) where you don't need to read the response.

options.label: Add `credentials: 'include'` to the fetch — CORS is blocking because cookies aren't sent
options.resultLog:
Browser console:
  Access to fetch at 'https://api.weather-service.example/current'
  from origin 'http://localhost:3000' has been blocked by CORS policy:
  No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header is present.

  TypeError: Failed to fetch

Same error. `credentials: 'include'` doesn't change the CORS response headers
the server sends. It tells the browser to send cookies with the request,
which the server needs to opt into separately (Access-Control-Allow-Credentials).
options.rationale: `credentials: 'include'` tells the browser to include cookies and auth tokens with cross-origin requests. It doesn't grant permission to make the request — that still requires the server to send `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`. In fact, with `credentials: 'include'`, CORS becomes stricter: the server must respond with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:3000` (not the wildcard `*`) AND `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`. Adding credentials without server-side support makes the CORS check fail more strictly, not less.

hints: CORS errors are blocked by the **browser**, based on headers the **server** sends. Frontend JavaScript can't bypass it — not with fetch options, not with headers on the request. The fix is on the server side.

The two real fixes: (1) the API server adds `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: your-origin` to responses, or (2) you create a backend proxy route on your own server that fetches the API server-to-server and returns the data.
explanationMd:
### What CORS is and why it exists
**CORS** (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser security policy. Its purpose: prevent malicious websites from reading your data from other sites. Without CORS, visiting a malicious page could make silent requests to your bank's API using your logged-in cookies, read the response, and steal your data.

The browser enforces this by checking cross-origin responses for `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` headers. If the header is missing (or doesn't include the requesting origin), the browser blocks the response — even if the server returned data normally.

### What 'cross-origin' means
An origin is the combination of protocol + domain + port: `http://localhost:3000`. A request is cross-origin if the URL it goes to has a different protocol, domain, or port:
- `http://localhost:3000` → `https://api.example.com` — different domain: cross-origin
- `http://localhost:3000` → `http://localhost:4000` — different port: cross-origin
- `http://app.example.com` → `https://api.example.com` — different protocol: cross-origin

### The two real fixes
**1. Server adds the header** (requires access to the API server):
```
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:3000
# or
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *  (allows any origin)
```

**2. Backend proxy** (when you can't modify the API server):
- Your browser fetches `/api/weather` (same origin — no CORS)
- Your server fetches `https://api.weather-service.example/current` (server-to-server — no CORS restriction, CORS is browser-only)
- Your server returns the data to the browser

### Why mode:'no-cors' doesn't work
`mode: 'no-cors'` creates an opaque response — the request is sent but the response is intentionally hidden. You can't read `response.ok`, `response.status`, or the body. It's designed for fire-and-forget requests (tracking pixels, analytics pings) where you don't need to read the response.

### Preflight requests
For some requests (POST with JSON, requests with custom headers), the browser sends an `OPTIONS` request first — the 'preflight'. If the server doesn't respond with the right CORS headers to the `OPTIONS`, the actual request never happens. This is why the Network tab sometimes shows an OPTIONS request with a CORS error.
recruiterReview: Correct. CORS is one of the most misunderstood browser features — the instinct is to fiddle with the frontend code, but the control is entirely server-side. The `no-cors` distractor is the most tempting wrong answer; it sounds like it bypasses the restriction but gives you an unreadable opaque response instead. The `credentials: 'include'` distractor is what someone might try if they've read about CORS auth — but it makes CORS stricter, not more permissive, without the matching server headers. The proxy pattern is the right answer when you don't control the API. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) Explained

### The same-origin policy
Browsers enforce a fundamental security rule: scripts on `http://site-a.com` cannot read responses from `http://site-b.com` by default. This is the **same-origin policy**. Without it, a malicious page could fetch your bank's homepage using your cookies and steal your account number.

An 'origin' is the combination of protocol + domain + port:
- `https://myapp.com` — origin A
- `https://api.myapp.com` — different subdomain — different origin
- `http://myapp.com` — different protocol — different origin
- `https://myapp.com:3000` — different port — different origin

### What CORS adds
CORS is a controlled relaxation of the same-origin policy. A server can say: 'I'm OK with site-a.com reading my responses' by adding a header:
```
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://site-a.com
```
or to allow anyone:
```
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *
```

Without this header, the browser **blocks** reading the response, even if the HTTP request completed successfully and returned data.

### Key facts about CORS
- **Enforced by the browser, not the server.** The server processes the request; CORS determines whether the browser gives your JavaScript access to the response.
- **Server-to-server has no CORS.** Only browsers implement it. curl, Node.js fetch, Python requests — none of them enforce CORS. That's why the same request 'works from curl' but fails from the browser.
- **Frontend code cannot bypass it.** `mode: 'no-cors'` doesn't bypass it — it hides the response. There's no JavaScript incantation that defeats CORS; it's enforced below the JavaScript layer.

### The two legitimate fixes
**Option 1: Update the API server headers**
If you control the API server, add the header to responses:
```js
// Express example:
app.use((req, res, next) => {
  res.header("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", "https://myapp.com");
  next();
});
```

**Option 2: Backend proxy**
If you don't control the API:
```
Browser → (same-origin) → /api/weather (your server)
                                          ↓ (server-to-server, no CORS)
                               https://external-api.com/data
```
Your backend fetches the external API and forwards the response. No CORS involved because CORS is browser-only.

### Preflight requests
Some requests trigger a 'preflight': the browser sends `OPTIONS` before the actual request to ask 'Are you willing to accept this type of request from this origin?' Simple GET requests with no custom headers usually don't preflight. POST with JSON body, or requests with `Authorization` headers, do. If the preflight fails (server doesn't respond correctly), the actual request never fires.

### You just learned
CORS is a browser-enforced same-origin security policy. The server controls access by sending `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` headers. Frontend JavaScript cannot bypass CORS — the fix is always server-side. For third-party APIs you don't control, use a backend proxy.
